result:
Every so often I find myself watching the quiet habits of neighborhood cats. They seem to understand the rhythms of the day better than any clock. At dawn they stretch across warm car hoods, looking toward the edges of town where the fields begin. By midday they lounge beneath hedges, eyes half closed but never quite sleeping. There’s a kind of independent patience in them: they wait without worrying about what comes next. That calm perspective feels useful, especially now. 

People move fast; news travels faster. Sometimes it feels impossible to keep up with the expectations tucked into every notification. The cats keep reminding me that attention can be gentle. They stare at birds they’ll never chase simply because watching is enough. I’ve started mimicking that mindset. When walking, I pick a single object—a leaf, a passing bicycle, a cloud—and follow it for a few moments, just to practice staying with one thing. 

It’s amazing how refreshing that becomes. Suddenly the day isn’t a checklist but a series of small scenes. The smell of baking bread near the market, the echo of footsteps in a tunnel, the contrast between sunlight and the shadow of a bus shelter—these details restore a sense of depth that multitasking strips away. You could call it mindfulness, yet it’s really just paying attention the way animals do. No app required, only the willingness to be still. The cats continue their rounds, graceful and unhurried. I try to take notes. They are marvelous teachers.

At dawn they stretch across warm car hoods, looking toward the edges of town where the fields begin. By midday they lounge beneath hedges, eyes half closed but never quite sleeping. There’s a kind of independent patience in them: they wait without worrying about what comes next. That calm perspective feels useful, especially now. 

People move fast; news travels faster. Sometimes it feels impossible to keep up with the expectations tucked into every notification. The cats keep reminding me that attention can be gentle. They stare at birds they’ll never chase simply because watching is enough. I’ve started mimicking that mindset. When walking, I pick a single object—a leaf, a passing bicycle, a cloud—and follow it for a few moments, just to practice staying with one thing. 

It’s amazing how refreshing that becomes. Suddenly the day isn’t a checklist but a series of small scenes. The smell of baking bread near the market, the echo of footsteps in a tunnel, the contrast between sunlight and the shadow of a bus shelter—these details restore a sense of depth that multitasking strips away. You could call it mindfulness, yet it’s really just paying attention the way animals do. No app required, only the willingness to be still. The cats continue their rounds, graceful and unhurried. I try to take notes. They are marvelous teachers.
